title: Bloody diarrhea with abdominal pain in an older adult

chart.oneLiner: 72M with cardiovascular disease presents with sudden crampy left-sided abdominal pain followed within hours by bloody diarrhea.

opening: I got this sudden cramp in my left side, and then I started passing bloody stool.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 72-year-old man, uncomfortable, anxious. Good historian.

HPI: Abrupt onset of crampy LEFT-sided abdominal pain, followed within a few hours by loose stools mixed with dark/bright blood. Recently had an episode of dehydration/low blood pressure from a stomach bug, and has known atherosclerotic disease (only if asked about recent illness/cardiovascular history). NO prior similar episodes, NO recent antibiotics, NO foreign travel.
PMH: hypertension, coronary artery disease, atrial fibrillation. MEDS: aspirin, metoprolol, a statin; anticoagulation status variable (if asked). SOCIAL: former smoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: uncomfortable but not toxic early.
- Vitals: BP 118/74, HR 98 (note rhythm if examined), RR 18, 37.6°C, SpO2 97% RA.
- Abdomen: tenderness over the LEFT/lower abdomen, mild; NO rigidity or rebound early (peritoneal signs would indicate transmural infarction).
- Rectal: blood present if examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the recent hypotension/dehydration or cardiovascular history unless asked about recent illness, blood pressure, or heart history.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: WBC 14.0 with left shift, Hgb 12.8, Plt 250.
labs.Lactate: 2.6 — mildly elevated (a markedly high lactate or peritonitis would suggest transmural/gangrenous disease).
labs.CT_abdomen: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: segmental colonic wall thickening in a watershed distribution (splenic flexure / descending colon); no free air or pneumatosis.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Atrial fibrillation (or sinus rhythm); no acute ST elevation. (AF/cardiovascular disease are risk factors.)
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'Atrial Fibrillation' library page — download an example tracing that matches: Atrial fibrillation (or sinus rhythm); no acute ST elevation. (AF/cardiovascular disease a … and self-host with credit to litfl.com.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: Atrial fibrillation with no acute ST elevation.

cxr.label: Abdominal X-ray (upright)
cxr.findings: Nonspecific; may show 'thumbprinting' (mucosal edema) of the affected colon; importantly NO free air (free air = perforation).
cxr.verify: Pick a CC abdominal film showing thumbprinting OR a normal/nonspecific film; must NOT show free air.
cxr.imageDescription: Abdominal radiograph with possible colonic thumbprinting and no free air.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Ischemic colitis (leading & CANT-MISS,8). Acute MESENTERIC ischemia — small-bowel, far more severe (CANT-MISS to distinguish,4). Infectious colitis (core,3). Inflammatory bowel disease (core,2). Diverticular bleed/diverticulitis (core,3). Connects sudden pain-then-bloody-diarrhea + vascular risk + recent hypotension to colonic ischemia (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Ischemic colitis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Acute MESENTERIC ischemia — small-bowel, far more severe. Also reasonable: Infectious colitis; Inflammatory bowel disease; Diverticular bleed/diverticulitis.

criticalActions.item: Acute MESENTERIC ischemia — small-bowel, far more severe

coreActions.item: Connects sudden pain-then-bloody-diarrhea + vascular risk + recent hypotension to colonic ischemia

mustNotMiss: Acute MESENTERIC ischemia — small-bowel, far more severe

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: CBC (2). Serum lactate (CANT-MISS,3). BMP (2). Stool studies to exclude infection (2). CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (CANT-MISS,4). EKG (1). Colonoscopy is diagnostic but performed after stabilization (1). PENALTY: shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; CBC; Lactate; BMP; Stool studies; CT abdomen/pelvis.

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = atrial fibrillation (or sinus); no acute STEMI. CREDIT: identifies the rhythm — AF vs sinus (6); links AF/cardiovascular disease as a risk factor for ischemia (4); no acute STEMI (3); systematic read (2). PENALTY: missing AF if present (-2).
keyFindings: identifies the rhythm — AF vs sinus
links AF/cardiovascular disease as a risk factor for ischemia
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation (or sinus rhythm); no acute ST elevation. (AF/cardiovascular disease are risk factors.)
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation (or sinus rhythm); no acute ST elevation. (AF/cardiovascular disease are risk factors.)

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Atrial fibrillation (or sinus rhythm); no acute ST elevation. (AF/cardiovascular disease are risk factors.)

coreActions.item: identifies the rhythm — AF vs sinus
links AF/cardiovascular disease as a risk factor for ischemia

steps.label: Read X-ray
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's abdominal film. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = nonspecific; possible thumbprinting; NO free air. CREDIT: identifies thumbprinting (mucosal edema) or a nonspecific film (5); explicitly excludes free air (perforation) (5). PENALTY: missing the free-air check (-1).
keyFindings: identifies thumbprinting (mucosal edema) or a nonspecific film
criticalFindings: Nonspecific; may show 'thumbprinting' (mucosal edema) of the affected colon; importantly NO free air (free air = perforation).
steps.idealAnswer: Nonspecific; may show 'thumbprinting' (mucosal edema) of the affected colon; importantly NO free air (free air = perforation).

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Nonspecific; may show 'thumbprinting' (mucosal edema) of the affected colon; importantly NO free air (free air = perforation).

coreActions.item: Identifies thumbprinting (mucosal edema) or a nonspecific film

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: CT shows segmental colonic wall thickening in a watershed area without perforation. What is your diagnosis and how does it differ from acute mesenteric ischemia?
steps.rubric: Ischemic colitis (6). Distinguishes from acute mesenteric ischemia — colonic (often watershed) and usually less catastrophic, vs small-bowel SMA occlusion with pain out of proportion and a worse prognosis (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Ischemic colitis. Unlike acute mesenteric ischemia, this is a colonic (often watershed, e.g., splenic flexure) process that is usually less catastrophic; acute mesenteric ischemia is a small-bowel SMA occlusion with pain out of proportion to exam and a far worse prognosis.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH approach (ischemic colitis): supportive care – bowel rest + IV fluid resuscitation (CANT-MISS,6); broad-spectrum antibiotics (4); optimize perfusion / stop vasoconstrictors (4); serial exams and lactate (3); surgery consult + CT if peritonitis/gangrene/perforation (4). PENALTY: missing transmural/gangrenous colitis needing surgery (-3); vasopressors worsening colonic ischemia without addressing cause (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Largely supportive: bowel rest, IV fluid resuscitation, and broad-spectrum antibiotics. Optimize perfusion – stop vasoconstrictors and minimize precipitants; correct hypotension. Serial abdominal exams and lactate. Surgical consult and CT for peritonitis, gangrene, perforation, or clinical deterioration. Most non-gangrenous cases resolve with supportive care.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Abdomen: tenderness over the LEFT/lower abdomen, mild; NO rigidity or rebound early (peritoneal signs would indicate transmural infarction).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 72M with cardiovascular disease presents with sudden crampy left-sided abdominal pain followed within hours by bloody diarrhea. The diagnosis is Ischemic colitis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: tenderness over the LEFT/lower abdomen, mild; NO rigidity or rebound early (peritoneal signs would indicate transmural infarction).
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Ischemic colitis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Ischemic colitis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.

Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.
